Vol. 7. pp. 271-280.

Printed in the USA. All rights resewed. 
0887-6185193 $6.00 + .oO

CLINICAL REPORT

Treatment of Separation Anxiety 
Disorder with Graduated Exposure and 
Reinforcement Targeting School 
Attendance: A Controlled Case Study

Lorns P. HACOPIAN 
AND KEITH J. SLIFER

Department of Behavioral Psychology, The Kennedy Krieger Institute and The Johns Hopkins 
University School of Medicine

Abstract - A six-year, nine-month old girl with school avoidance and a variety of 
other anxiety symptoms was diagnosed with Separation Anxiety Disorder (SAD). 
SAD was treated using graduated exposure with positive reinforcement for approxi- 
mations of independent school attendance. Treatment focused on systematically shap- 
ing increased duration of school attendance while fading mother’s proximity and time 
spent in the classroom and in transit to and from school. A changing criterion single- 
subject research design was used to demonstrate functional control of the treatment 
over the child’s school attendance. Follow-up indicated that treatment gains were 
maintained at two- and nine-months post-treatment. SAD is discussed as a childhood 
disorder involving fear and avoidance of separation from parents in a variety of situa- 
tions, one of which may be attending school, The importance of differentiating SAD 
from school phobia and other anxiety disorders is discussed.

For young children, 
distress and anxiety upon separation 
from a parent or 
other attachment 
figure is developmentally 
normal 
and usually 
transient. 
When anxiety 
in response 
to separation 
or the anticipation 
of separation 
is 
excessive 
and persists over time, Separation 
Anxiety Disorder (SAD) is said 
to be present. Children 
diagnosed 
with SAD avoid separation 
from their par- 
ent (or other attachment 
figure), frequently 
express concerns 
that they or their 
parent 
may be harmed 
when separated, 
and often panic upon separation 
(American 
Psychiatric 
Association, 
1987).

The authors wish to thank Thomas 1~. Ollendick for his help and advice in the preparation of this 
manuscript. 
Correspondence should be directed to Louis I? Hagopian, Ph.D., Deparnnent of Behavioral 
Psychology, The Kennedy Krieger Institute, 707 N. Broadway, Baltimore, MD 21205. 
271

272 
L.P. HAGOPIAN AND K.J. SLIFER

As is the case with all anxiety-based 
disorders 
in children, 
there is much 
controversy 
over SAD as a diagnostic 
category (Ollendick, 
1983; Silverman, 
1987; Thyer & Sowers-Hoag, 
1988). In particular, 
SAD has been historically 
confused 
with school phobia, 
making 
it difficult 
to interpret 
much of the 
research. Recently, a case has been made that SAD and school phobia should 
be distinguished 
from one another 
(Last, Francis 
et al., 1987; Ollendick 
& 
Mayer, 1984; Thyer & Sowers-Hoag, 
1988). According 
to this position, 
SAD 
involves 
fear and avoidance 
of a variety of situations 
involving 
the common 
theme of separation 
(with school avoidance 
being only one of a number 
of 
avoided situations). 
School phobia, on the other hand, involves 
fear of specific 
stimuli related to the school situation 
itself (such as fear of other children 
at 
school, ridicule, 
or criticism 
by teachers; 
see Klein & Last, 1989). School 
avoidance 
may also be a function of other problems such as depression 
or con- 
duct disorder, which further supports the position that a careful assessment 
is 
critical in formulating 
the most appropriate intervention. 
In addition to the difficulties 
associated with the failure to differentiate 
SAD 
from other problems 
(such as school phobia or other anxiety disorders), 
other 
methodological 
concerns regarding the treatment literature on SAD have been 
noted (Klein & Last, 1989). The majority of the research has been conducted 
using uncontrolled 
case studies; controlled 
group and comparative 
studies are 
lacking; and the use of oqjective, 
standardized 
assessment 
procedures 
to eval- 
uate treatment 
outcome 
is infrequent. 
Thyer 
and Sowers-Hoag 
(1988) 
reviewed 
the behavioral 
treatment 
studies 
where separation 
anxiety 
was 
“clearly described as a presenting 
problem” 
(p. 218). Unfortunately, 
for many 
of the cases they describe, 
it is not entirely 
clear whether school phobia or 
other anxieties 
were present in addition 
to separation 
anxiety. According 
to 
their review, behavioral 
treatments 
for SAD generally employ techniques 
sim- 
ilar to those used in the treatment of anxiety in adults. Exposure to the feared 
stimulus is recognized 
as a critical element in the treatment of anxiety (Marks, 
1987). The treatment 
studies of SAD reviewed 
by Thyer and Sowers-Hoag 
(1988) included 
interventions 
involving 
shaping, 
systematic 
desensitization, 
parent training, 
graduated exposure, 
and relaxation 
training. 
For the majority 
of behaviorally-based 
treatments described, exposure to separation was a com- 
ponent of the treatment 
package. The authors reported positive outcomes 
for 
the studies reviewed; 
however, the majority 
of studies did not provide 
ade- 
quate experimental 
control. 
In a recent study, Ollendick 
et al. (1991) reported on the treatment 
of two 
cases of SAD using a multiple baseline-across 
subjects design. For both these 
cases, which met DSM-III-R 
criteria 
for SAD using structured 
diagnostic 
interviews, 
nighttime 
fears were the primary complaint, 
whereas school avoid- 
ance was not a problem. 
A reinforcement 
and self-control 
training 
program 
targeting 
sleeping 
apart from their mothers 
was successful 
in gradually 
increasing 
the childrens’ 
sleeping in their own beds. Although 
other separa- 
tion-related 
anxieties were not targeted by the treatment, neither child met cri- 
teria for SAD at post-treatment 
or follow-up. 
Here we report on a young girl for whom school avoidance 
was the most 
prominent 
and disruptive 
feature of her separation 
anxiety. Although 
she had 
a host of other fears and concerns, 
school avoidance 
was selected as the tar

TREATMENT 
OF SEPARATION 
ANXImY 
DISORDER 
AND SCHOOL 
AVOIDANCE 
273

get of treatment, 
because this was the most problematic 
feature of her separa- 
tion anxiety.
METHOD
Case Description

Jennifer, a six-year, nine-month 
old white female, had a history of petit mal 
seizures, 
which had been well controlled 
with Zarontin 
since she was five 
years old. The presenting 
concerns were Jennifer’s refusal to stay in her class- 
room without one of her parents present, excessive 
concerns 
about her par- 
ents’ well-being, 
and concerns 
about herself becoming 
ill. Jennifer 
lived with 
her parents and two-year old brother, and was enrolled in the fist grade. She 
was verbal, socially skilled, and proved to be an excellent historian 
during the 
clinical interviews. 
The school avoidance 
began when she returned to school after an extended 
holiday break. At that time, Jennifer began reporting numerous somatic concerns 
prior to leaving for school. Within a few days, she began clinging to her parents, 
crying, shaking, pleading 
for them to stay, and chasing after them when they 
attempted to leave her at school. On one occasion, Jennifer kicked the principal 
when she attempted to intervene. 
Eventually, Jennifer’s mother (sometimes her 
father) was taking her to school and staying in her class every day. Jennifer 
would typically bc taken home after a few hours, so her mother could go to work. 
According 
to both Jennifer and her mother, she began expressing 
concerns 
about becoming 
ill approximately 
six months prior to her tirst appointment. 
At 
that time, Jennifer 
had a stomach virus, which she presumably 
caught from 
her brother. She continued 
to avoid many of the foods that she had vomited 
while she was ill. In addition, Jennifer began repeatedly 
asking her brother if 
he felt ill every night, sometimes 
having her mother waking him to ask him 
how he felt. Jennifer had always slept with her mother and brother (the father 
worked at night). Although 
this was not one of the parents’ presenting 
con- 
cerns, her mother reported that Jennifer refused to sleep elsewhere. 
Upon coming 
home early from school, Jennifer 
was allowed 
to play or 
watch television. 
Although 
she did not have difficulty 
staying at home with 
her aunt after leaving school early, she called her mother at work several times 
a day. She engaged in avoidant behaviors 
similar to those reported at school 
when either one of her parents left the house in the evenings 
or on weekends, 
and when her mother attempted 
to take her to Brownies 
meetings. 
In dis- 
cussing 
her concerns 
about her mother, Jennifer 
stated “I always want to be 
with her in case something 
happens to her.” She reported concerns 
that her 
mother might be in an automobile 
accident, and added that this had occurred 
two years prior. Her mother acknowledged 
that she had been in an accident, 
but indicated 
that it was very minor. These separation 
avoidant behaviors 
had 
persisted for approximately 
10 weeks, until she was first seen in our clinic.
Assessment

A multimodal 
assessment 
similar 
to that described 
by Hagopian 
et al. 
(1990) was conducted. 
A number 
of self- and parent-report 
questionnaires

274 
L. P. HAGOPIAN AND K. J. SLIFER

were administered 
to both Jennifer 
and her mother, and behavioral 
observa- 
tions were conducted. 
Separate behavioral 
and clinical-diagnostic 
interviews 
were conducted 
individually 
with Jennifer 
and her mother by a single thera- 
pist. During the diagnostic interviews, 
both mother and child were asked ques- 
tions about the presence 
or absence 
of specific 
symptoms 
of the Anxiety 
Disorders 
of Childhood 
or Adolescence, 
Dysthymia, 
and Major Depressive 
Disorder 
listed 
in the Diagnostic 
and Statistical 
Manual 
(American 
Psychiatric 
Asspciation, 
1987). 
Jennifer 
met DSM-III-R 
criteria 
for Separation 
Anxiety 
Disorder 
(SAD) 
based on her report and that of her mother, meeting six of the nine DSM-III-R 
criteria (persistent 
worry about possible harm befalling 
mother, refusal to go 
to school, refusal to go to sleep without mother, physical complaints 
upon sep- 
aration, 
distress in anticipation 
of separation, 
distress upon separation; 
only 
three are required for a diagnosis). 
The school avoidance 
was not a function of 
a school phobia, since Jennifer did not express fears or concerns about school 
apart from separating 
from her parents, and her separation 
avoidant behavior 
was not limited to the school situation. 
As discussed earlier, such differences 
are viewed 
as necessary 
for the differentiation 
of school phobia from SAD 
(Last, Francis et al., 1987). 
Significant 
elevations 
in the Depressed 
and Somatic 
Complaints 
factors 
were evident 
on the Child 
Behavior 
Checklist 
(CBCL; 
Achenbach 
& 
Edelbrock, 
1983; see Table 1). These elevations 
were consistent 
with a num- 
ber of disorders 
(e.g., overanxious, 
major 
depression, 
dysthymia 
- 
see 
Achenbach, 
1991); 
however, 
when considered 
along 
with information 
obtained 
in the interviews, 
Jennifer 
did not meet criteria for these disorders. 
Ratings on all other factors were in the normal range. Based on the informa- 
tion from the CBCL and interviews, 
disorders 
of conduct, 
attention, 
and 
development 
were ruled out. Jennifer obtained a slightly elevated score of 20 
on the Revised 
Children’s 
Manifest 
Anxiety 
Scale (RCMAS; 
Reynolds 
& 
Richmond, 
1978). Her score of 6 on the Children’s 
Depression 
Inventory 
(CDI; Kovacs, 
1978) was low, and was consistent 
with the interview 
data, 
which indicated that depression was not present. Mother’s scores on the State- 
Trait Anxiety Inventory 
(STAI; Speilberger, 
1973) Trait form, and on the Beck 
Depression 
Inventory 
(BDI; Beck et al., 1979) were in the normal range, and 
family history of anxiety and other psychiatric 
disorder were not reported.
Pre-Baseline

For the two weeks before the initial session, the mother was instructed 
to 
keep a written narrative 
report of Jennifer’s 
separation 
avoidant 
and anxious 
behaviors 
when she attempted to leave her at school. She was also instructed 
to continue 
to respond to Jennifer 
during this pre-baseline 
phase as she had 
been in the past weeks.
Baseline

During 
the course of the assessment, 
a two and one-half 
week baseline 
phase was conducted. 
During 
this phase, 
separation 
was not attempted.

TREATMENT OF SEPARATION ANXIETY DISORDER AND SCHOOL AVOIDANCE

‘denotes a sxse outside the NomA Range on the CBCL.

Jennifer’s 
mother continued 
to take Jennifer to school, but remained 
in class 
with her for one hour. Mother continued 
to record Jennifer’s 
avoidant behav- 
ior and anxious episodes.
Treatment

Jennifer’s 
refusal to stay in her class without one of her parents present was 
selected as the primary 
treatment 
target because of deterioration 
in her aca- 
demic performance 
and the financial 
consequences 
of her mother’s absence 
from work. Furthermore, 
it was hypothesized 
that treatment effects in this sit- 
uation might generalize 
to the other high-anxiety 
situations. 
Treatment 
involved 
graduated 
exposure 
to separation 
from mother 
with 
reinforcement 
for appropriate 
behavior upon separation. 
Using a changing 
cri- 
terion design to demonstrate 
functional 
control (Barlow & Hersen, 1984) the 
amount of time Jennifer was required to stay in her class without mother was 
gradually increased. 
Criteria for time in class without mother, as well as inter- 
action 
with and proximity 
to mother, 
were faded concurrently. 
Initially, 
Jennifer’s 
mother was instructed 
to take her to school and leave four times 
during the hour during which they were at school, leaving for only 30 seconds 
at a time. Over the course 
of treatment, 
Jennifer’s 
mother 
increased 
the 
amount of time and distance she was away while Jennifer remained 
in class.

276 
L. P. HAGOPIAN 
AND K. J. SLIFER

The bases for the decision to proceed to the next criterion were Jennifer’s 
suc- 
cess in meeting the current criterion at least two times, as well as her own and 
her mother’s 
level of comfort with proceeding 
to the next criterion. 
Mother 
gradually 
went from standing 
outside the door, to walking 
down the hall, to 
sitting in her car, to driving to work, to not returning 
at all, and finally to not 
taking Jennifer to or from school. 
The reinforcement 
component, 
which involved 
providing 
Jennifer a sticker 
(after school) for each time she remained 
in her seat while mother was out of 
the room (there was no response 
cost for failed trials), was initiated 
on the 
fourth day of treatment. 
Stickers could be exchanged 
for prizes at the end of 
each week. 
Much praise 
and encouragement 
were provided 
to Jennifer 
throughout 
treatment, 
and positive self-statements 
were encouraged 
(although 
not formally trained). An additional 
treatment component 
was implemented 
at 
home upon Jennifer’s 
early return from school. She was required to do school 
work until after lunch, after which time she could play as she wished. 
In addition, some general recommendations 
for dealing with anxious behav- 
ior at home were made to Jennifer’s 
parents. They were instructed 
to provide 
minimal 
attention 
to Jennifer’s 
queries about the safety of foods or about her 
parents’ 
well-being, 
and to ignore 
excessive 
inappropriate 
crying. 
Once 
Jennifer 
was attending 
school more regularly, 
a reinforcement 
treatment 
pro- 
gram was initiated to increase Jennifer’s 
sleeping in her own bed. This proce- 
dure involved 
presenting 
Jennifer with a sticker and social praise the morning 
after she successfully 
slept in her own bed.
RESULTS

For the two weeks prior to their initial session during the pre-baseline 
phase, 
mother’s written narrative revealed that when she attempted to leave her in her 
class, Jennifer would “panic” and follow her out into the hallway, often crying, 
shaking, 
and pleading 
for mother to stay. After attempting 
to leave multiple 
times per day, Jennifer’s 
mother would eventually 
take her home. Jennifer 
immediately 
followed mother out of class every time she attempted to leave the 
class, except on two occasions, where Jennifer remained in class for brief peri- 
ods (she had to be restrained by the teacher on one of these occasions). During 
the brief periods of time Jennifer was in school without mother, she was report- 
edly very anxious, and attempted to call her mother. 
The amount of time Jennifer 
spent in class without mother over the course 
of the assessment 
and intervention 
phases is presented in Fig. 1. As instructed, 
Jennifer’s 
mother did not attempt to leave the class during the baseline 
phase 
conducted 
over the course of the assessment. 
Jennifer’s 
mother reported that 
Jennifer 
did not become anxious, but frequently 
asked her mother to sit with 
her and join her in activities. 
For the first three days of treatment, 
Jennifer 
continued 
to follow mother 
immediately 
each time mother left the classroom. 
After the addition 
of the 
reinforcement 
component 
for staying in her seat when mother left, Jennifer 
remained 
in class each time mother left. She met the majority of the changing 
time and proximity 
criteria over the next six weeks. During the fifth week of 
treatment, 
when the criterion was increased to having Jennifer ride the bus to

TREAThtENTOFSEPARATlONANXIETYDlSORDERANDSCHOOLAVOlDANCE 
277
FIG. ~.JENNIFER'S~?MEINCLASS AND BROWNIES MEETINGSW~OU'I'MOTHERPRESENT.

school, she refused. 
After mother rode the bus with her the next two days 
(without 
sitting 
with Jennifer), 
Jennifer 
successfully 
rode the bus without 
mother thereafter. This was maintained 
until the end of school year, and the 
reinforcement 
component 
was faded out during the final week. 
Following 
the introduction 
of the reinforcement 
program 
for Jennifer’s 
sleeping in her own bed, improvements 
were immediately 
evident. On the first 
night of treatment, Jennifer was required to stay in her own bed for two hours, 
but remained 
the entire night. She did not demonstrate 
any difficulties 
sleep- 
ing in her own bed thereafter. The stickers were gradually faded and discontin- 
ued one week before school ended.

Post-treatment 
and Follow-up

At post-treatment 
as well as two- and nine-month 
follow-up, 
both Jennifer 
and mother scored in the normal range on all measures (see Table 1). Jennifer 
was attending 
school 
regularly 
by the end of treatment, 
and at her own 
request, 
returned 
to Brownies 
meetings 
and attended 
day camp during 
the 
summer. 
She no longer met any of the DSM-III-R 
criteria 
for SAD or any 
other disorder 
based on diagnostic 
interviews 
conducted 
with her and her 
mother separately by the same therapist who conducted 
the initial assessments 
and treatment. 
At nine-month 
follow-up, 
her school attendance 
during the next academic 
year remained 
high, and she continued 
to participate 
in Brownies 
meetings 
without difficulties 
(see Fig. 1). During this time, the contingencies 
present 
during treatment 
were not in effect. Jennifer was absent only three days (for 
legitimate 
reasons) 
during each of the 45day 
quarters after treatment, 
com- 
pared to the 19 inappropriate 
absences during the quarter prior to treatment.

278 
L.P. HAGOPIAN AND K.1. SLIFER

Jennifer 
also continued 
to sleep in her own bed without difficulty, 
did not 
call her mother at work, was eating school and camp lunches (including 
foods 
she had previously 
avoided), and no longer expressed worries about her own 
or her mother’s health. She referred to her past fears and excessive 
concerns 
about her mother as the time when “I was acting crazy.”
DISCUSSION

The treatment 
package 
involving 
graduated 
exposure 
and reinforcement 
was effective in increasing 
Jennifer’s school attendance 
and in eliminating 
her 
separation 
anxieties. 
The changing 
criterion 
design demonstrated 
functional 
control of the treatment 
over Jennifer’s 
time in her class without her mother 
present. Furthermore, 
the treatment allowed graduated exposure to separation, 
as well as systematic 
shaping of both Jennifer’s 
and her mother’s behavior. 
Given 
that parents 
of children 
with SAD are often anxious 
themselves 
(Last, Hersen, 
Kazdin, Francis, 
& Grubb, 
1987), changing 
parental behavior 
may be especially 
critical in the treatment 
of SAD in children. 
For children 
with SAD, expression 
of concerns 
about their parent’s 
(or their own) well- 
being often results in obtaining 
reassurance 
from the parent. Refusal to stay in 
class or sleep alone, along with crying and pleading, 
often results in school 
avoidance 
or sleeping 
with the parent, respectively. 
Indeed, parents of chil- 
dren with SAD often go to great lengths to quell their children’s 
concerns, 
fears, and sometimes 
embarrassing 
avoidant 
behaviors. 
These 
seemingly 
indulgent 
parental behaviors may be negatively 
reinforced 
by an anxious child 
becoming 
calm. Since graduated exposure may be less distressing 
for both the 
child and parent, such an approach may be particularly 
important 
in the treat- 
ment of SAD. 
In addition to adding to a limited SAD treatment literature, the present case 
study demonstrates 
the utility of a changing 
criterion 
design. The changing 
criterion 
design may be especially 
well suited for interventions 
using graduat- 
ed exposure 
to the feared stimulus, 
since gradual progression 
is an inherent 
element of both the design and treatment protocol. In addition, it allows for a 
systematic 
demonstration 
of the effects of reinforcement 
on shaping approach 
behavior. 
Such a design may be more appropriate 
than reversal designs, since 
a reversal may interfere with the extinction 
processes assumed to occur during 
exposure. 
Furthermore, 
in cases where skill acquisition 
is a component 
of the 
treatment 
package (e.g., parent training, 
self-instructional 
strategies), 
demon- 
stration of functional 
control using a reversal design may not be possible 
or 
appropriate 
(Barlow & Hersen, 1984). 
Although 
the multimodal 
assessment 
using behavioral 
and clinical diagnos- 
tic interviews 
and standardized 
questionnaires 
indicated 
a diagnosis 
of SAD, 
the failure to use a structured diagnostic 
interview 
does not allow for the reli- 
able confirmation 
of the diagnosis of SAD or the exclusion 
of other diagnoses. 
Furthermore, 
it is not possible to rule out experimenter 
bias, as the same thera- 
pist conducted 
the assessment, 
treatment, 
and follow-up. 
The present study is 
also limited by the fact that it reports on only a single case, making it difficult 
to generalize 
the findings 
to other individuals, 
settings, etc. In addition, 
it is 
difficult to draw conclusions 
about the relative importance 
of the different ele-

TREATMENT OF SEPARATION ANXIETY DISORDER AND SCHOOL AVOIDANCE 
279

ments of the intervention 
because a formal components 
analysis was not con- 
ducted. Finally, 
a multiple-baseline 
across settings 
design might have been 
useful in more systematically 
demonsuating 
that Jennifer’s 
reduced separation 
avoidant 
behavior 
at school 
generalized 
to other settings 
and situations. 
Completion 
of group and comparative 
studies using standardized 
assessment 
instruments 
and structured 
diagnostic 
interviews 
remains 
a challenge 
for 
researchers 
examining 
treatments 
for SAD and other anxiety-based 
disorders 
in children. 
Differentiation 
of SAD from school phobia and other problems 
that may 
result in school avoidance 
is important 
both in terms of improving 
the status 
of the literature 
and in designing 
interventions. 
In the present study, the focus 
of treatment 
was on Jennifer’s 
separation 
from mother. One reason that the 
school setting was selected was because Jennifer’s 
difficulty 
separating 
from 
her parents at school was the most problematic 
feature of her separation 
anxi- 
ety. Initially, 
this may seem to contradict 
the position 
that differentiation 
of 
SAD from school phobia is important 
because the treatment 
described 
here 
appears similar 
to a treatment 
for school phobia. Had her ,fears been more 
related to the school situation 
itself, however, 
careful 
identification 
of and 
exposure 
to those particular 
stimuli would have been the focus of treatment 
(see Mansdorf 
& Lukens, 
1987). Similarly, 
had nighttime 
problems 
been 
more disruptive 
than school avoidance, 
sleeping apart from her parents might 
have been targeted first (see Ollendick 
et al., 1991). In the present study, and 
in the two cases described 
by Ollendick 
et al. (1991), 
such an approach 
appeared to result in the generalized 
reduction 
of separation 
anxieties 
across 
other settings and stimuli. Although SAD generally 
has widespread 
effects on 
the child and family, it may be more efficient to focus treatment 
on the situa- 
tion in which the separation anxieties are most prominent 
and disruptive 
to the 
child and family.

therapy with an ll-

using DSM-III criteria. American Journnl of Psychiatry,

the mothers of anxious children. American Journal of Psychiatry, 144, 1580-1583.
